title: Exodus 22
subtitle: Property Laws; Social and Religious Laws; Care for the Vulnerable

sections.header: Verses 1—15 — Laws on Property: Theft, Restitution, Negligence

heb.paragraph: V.1-17: The property laws cover theft, damage to fields and flocks, and deposit violations. The use of silver (kesep) as monetary unit throughout these laws reflects a market economy within which covenant principles operate. Proportional restitution — two, four, or fivefold depending on the offense — reflects both punishment and deterrence. Paul's principle "Let no debt remain outstanding" (Rom 13:8) applies the same restitution logic to relational obligations.

V.21: "Do not mistreat or oppress a foreigner (gēr), for you were foreigners in Egypt." This law of protection for the gēr — resident alien without full citizenship — is repeated more than 30 times in the Pentateuch. The foundation is always experiential: "you were strangers (gēr)." Israel's identity as former slaves who were strangers grounds the ethical obligation to protect the vulnerable other. Eph 2:19 applies this: Christians are "no longer foreigners and strangers, but fellow citizens with God's people."

hist.historical: The property laws on theft and restitution have parallels in the Code of Hammurabi (laws 6–25) and the Hittite laws. The principle of multiple restitution (four or five times for livestock, double for other property) serves as both punishment and deterrent. Oath-taking before the LORD (v. 11) to resolve disputes where no witnesses exist is attested in Mesopotamian judicial procedure — the 'oath of the gods' was considered binding and self-enforcing through divine punishment of perjury.
hist.context: The care-for-the-vulnerable laws (vv.21–27) are grounded in three foundations: (1) historical memory — "you were strangers"; (2) theological accountability — "if they cry to me, I will hear"; (3) divine character — "I am compassionate." The laws are not humanitarian sentimentalism; they are the overflow of the covenant's core values: the God who heard Israel's cry in Egypt hears the cry of the widow and orphan in Israel. Ignoring their cry is to act against the character of God himself.

refs.note: "You shall treat the stranger who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt."
"Religion that is pure and undefiled before God the Father is this: to visit orphans and widows in their affliction." James applies Exod 22:22–23.
The judgment of the nations on how they treated "the least of these" — the widow/orphan/stranger criteria of Exod 22 become the eschatological criterion.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The restitution values (five for an ox, four for a sheep) reflect their relative economic importance in the agrarian economy. Sarna notes that the ox was the primary farm tool — losing an ox was losing productive capacity for the entire agricultural season. The fourfold/fivefold restitution (rather than simple return) serves as deterrence proportional to the economic damage of the crime.

The cases involving disputed property left in a neighbour's keeping (vv.7–9) establish the legal framework of bailment — the deposit of property with another for safekeeping. Sarna notes these laws are remarkably sophisticated: they distinguish between simple theft by the bailee, claimed theft by third parties (requiring oath), and disputed ownership (requiring judicial determination). The laws anticipate modern contract and tort law by three millennia.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The law allowing lethal force against a burglar at night but not by day is one of the Book of the Covenant's most legally precise distinctions. Alter notes the reasoning: at night, a householder cannot know the burglar's intent or see the danger clearly — lethal defence is understandable. By day, the situation is visible and less threatening — the householder is 'guilty of bloodshed' if he kills a daytime intruder. Intent and context matter in the covenant law.

The progressive liability structure — thief caught with animal pays double; animal found alive pays double; theft not recovered by thief must be proven by oath — establishes a graduated system in which the penalty fits the specific form of the wrong. Alter notes the detail reflects the practical legal problems of a livestock-based economy.

Calvin on restitution law: 'The thief does not merely restore what he has taken, but makes fourfold or fivefold satisfaction... for the law will not allow him to profit by his injustice.' The principle is not merely restoration but consequence — the thief is made worse off by the theft than if he had not stolen. Calvin reads this as proportional deterrence: the penalty must outweigh the gain of the crime.

On the oath before the LORD: 'God is brought in as the judge between them, before whom the conscience of the innocent man will stand firm, while the guilty one will be terror-stricken.' The divine oath serves both as evidence (invoking the divine witness) and as moral pressure (the guilty person cannot comfortably swear falsely before God). The covenant community's justice system works through conscience as well as procedure.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'before God' (el ha'Elohim) in legal disputes discusses whether this means before a judge (Elohim used for judges, cf. 21:6), before a divine oracle (Urim and Thummim), or before God directly in an oath. The note favours the judge-as-divine-representative reading — the human judge functions as God's representative in the legal process. Either way, the legal dispute is elevated to a theological act.

The NET note on the sacred oath before God discusses how 'el haElohim' (before God) functioned in Israelite legal procedure: the parties appeared at the sanctuary where the oath was sworn before the divine judge. The note discusses whether this refers to an actual sanctuary visit or a metaphorical divine witness invocation.

MacArthur: Whoever steals an ox must pay back five head of cattle. The restitution requirement reflects the covenant principle that theft's harm to the community requires more than mere return -- it requires genuine redress.

MacArthur: The borrowing laws establish graduated liability based on the relationship of the borrower to the owner. Those who borrow must be responsible for what they borrow -- stewardship carries accountability.

sections.header: Verses 16—31 — Social Laws: Seduction, Sorcery, Oppression, Justice

heb.paragraph: V.26-27: "If you take your neighbor's cloak as a pledge, return it by sunset... When he cries out to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate (ḥannûn ʾănî)." The cloak law embodies ḥesed in legislation: the basic human need of the poor (warmth at night) overrides the legal entitlement of the creditor. God himself supervenes as the guarantor of the poor's cry: "I will hear." This is the covenant loyalty principle made concrete.

V.22-24: "Do not take advantage of a widow or an orphan (yātôm)." The widow and orphan (yātôm ûʾalmānāh) are the paradigmatic vulnerable people in ancient society — without male household heads to advocate for them. Their protection becomes the test of societal justice throughout the prophets (Isa 1:17; Jer 22:3; Zech 7:10) and the NT: "Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress" (Jas 1:27).

hist.historical: The social laws protecting the vulnerable — strangers, widows, orphans — are distinctive in their theological grounding ('for you were strangers in Egypt'). While Mesopotamian codes mention protection of the weak (Hammurabi's epilogue), the Israelite laws root social justice in historical memory and divine character. The prohibition of sorcery reflects the distinction between legitimate priestly divination (Urim and Thummim) and illegitimate magical practices. The ban on interest-charging (v. 25) applies to fellow Israelites and addresses subsistence lending, not commercial credit.
hist.context: The theft laws (vv.1–15) establish restitution as the primary mechanism of justice — not imprisonment but restoration. The restitution multiples (five oxen for an ox, four sheep for a sheep) both compensate the victim and serve as deterrent. The seduction law (vv.16–17) protects the woman's future by requiring the man to pay the bride price whether or not she chooses to marry him. These laws reveal a concern for the victim's restoration, not merely the offender's punishment.

refs.note: Love your neighbour as yourself — the summary of the social laws of Exod 22.
Paul quotes Exod 22:28 (do not curse a ruler) when confronted with the high priest, showing its continued applicability in the NT period.
Do not avenge yourselves — the restitution principle of Exod 22 transformed into NT ethics.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The foreigner-protection command is unique in the ancient world — no other ancient law code specifically protects resident aliens. Sarna notes the 36 separate OT commands to protect the ger (resident alien) make this the most legislated ethical requirement in the Torah. The theological ground is always the same: 'for you were strangers in Egypt' (22:21; 23:9; Lev 19:34; Deut 10:19). Memory of oppression creates ethical obligation.

The prohibition of interest on loans to the poor (lo titen lo neshek) is radical in the ancient economy, where interest-bearing loans were standard. Sarna notes that in the ancient Near East, interest rates of 20–33% were common. Israel's law prohibits charging interest to fellow Israelites in need (distinguished from commercial loans to foreigners in Deut 23:20). The poor person's loan is a covenant obligation of generosity, not a commercial transaction.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The section on widow, orphan, foreigner, and poor moves from civil law to prophetic ethics. Alter notes the shift in tone: earlier laws use the third-person case form ('if a man...'); these laws use second-person address ('you shall not...') with direct divine threat ('my wrath will burn'). The most vulnerable members of society receive the most direct divine protection. The law's emotional register rises when the law addresses those with no legal advocate.

The prohibition on charging interest to the poor and on keeping the poor person's garment overnight are not merely humanitarian recommendations but covenant obligations. Alter notes the theological grounding in v.27: 'When he cries out to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate.' Divine responsiveness to the poor's cry is both warning and promise.

Calvin on the foreigner law: 'By the example of their own condition, they were reminded to exercise kindness toward strangers... for it is a feeling natural to all, to dislike strangers; and hence it is that almost everywhere they are treated unkindly.' Calvin takes the law's psychological realism seriously: the natural tendency to exclude foreigners must be actively resisted by the discipline of memory. The covenant law works against natural self-preference.

On the cloak-pledge: 'It is as if God had said that he was the protector of the poor, and that their complaints would not be without effect.' The theological claim is striking: God is the legal advocate of the poor man who has no court to appeal to. Calvin uses this to establish the principle that divine justice compensates for human legal failure — when courts do not protect the vulnerable, God does.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'that is his only covering, it is his cloak for his body' explains the economic reality: in the ancient world, a poor person's outer garment (simla) served as both daytime clothing and nighttime blanket. Taking it permanently as a loan pledge was equivalent to removing the person's bed. The law's specificity reflects knowledge of actual poverty — the legislator knows what it is to be cold without a blanket.

The NET note on the cluster of laws closing the chapter — not cursing God or the ruler, offering firstfruits, giving firstborn sons, avoiding carrion — discusses how these varied commands are held together by the holiness principle: the covenant community is 'holy people' (v.31) whose everyday practices express that status.

MacArthur: Do not take advantage of a widow or an orphan. If they cry out to me, I will certainly hear their cry. The direct divine sanction for oppression of the vulnerable is among the most severe in the Torah.

MacArthur: Give me the firstborn of your sons. The firstborn offering acknowledges that all productivity belongs to God who gives it. The offering of first-fruits reflects the covenant's priority: give to God first.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"You shall not wrong a sojourner or oppress him, for you were sojourners in the land of Egypt."NIV"Do not mistreat or oppress a foreigner, for you were foreigners in Egypt."NASB"You shall not wrong a stranger or oppress him, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt."

src.title: ANE Widow and Orphan Laws
src.quote: Both Hammurabi's code and Ugaritic texts include provisions for widows and orphans, showing the universal recognition of their vulnerability. Israel's laws are distinctive in grounding the obligation in divine character rather than royal benevolence.
src.note: The protection of widows and orphans is a universal ANE concern; Exodus grounds it in the character of God himself.

src.title: Alien Protection
src.quote: Egyptian texts document the complex status of foreign residents. Israel's legislation is unusual in the ancient world for the extent of protection extended to aliens — grounded in the covenantal memory of Egypt.
src.note: The gēr laws have no precise equivalent in any other ANE legal tradition.

rec.text: The prophets use Exod 22's language to indict Israel for selling the poor and oppressing the stranger — Exodus 22 becomes the prophetic standard for social justice.
The NT's application of the widow/orphan care principle — "pure religion" defined by Exod 22:22.

rows.label: Widow, Orphan, Foreigner: The Covenant's Vulnerable Triangle
rows.text: The three-fold command: do not oppress the foreigner, widow, or orphan. The theological grounding: if they cry to me, I will hear them. The social ethics are not humanitarian sentiment but covenant theology.

rows.label: You Know How It Feels: Empathy as Ethical Foundation
rows.text: Do not oppress a foreigner; you yourselves know how it feels to be foreigners in Egypt. The covenant community's experience of injustice is the ethical ground for their obligation to the vulnerable.

Love your neighbour as yourself — the summary of the social laws of Exod 22.
Paul quotes Exod 22:28 (do not curse a ruler) when confronted with the high priest, showing its continued applicability in the NT period.

tx.title: 'You shall not revile God' — ʾĕlōhîm ambiguity
tx.content: MT reads 'you shall not curse ʾĕlōhîm' — which can mean 'God' or 'judges/rulers.' The LXX translates as θεούς ('gods'), reading a prohibition against cursing pagan deities. The Vulgate reads 'diis' ('gods'). Targum Onqelos reads 'judges.' Acts 23:5 applies it to human authorities.
tx.note: This is one of the most theologically consequential translation decisions in the Book of the Covenant. The LXX's plural reading implies religious tolerance; the Targum's implies judicial respect.

debate.title: The Book of the Covenant and Ancient Near Eastern Law Codes

positions.name: Dependent on Hammurabi's Code
positions.argument: Several laws in the Book of the Covenant have close parallels in Hammurabi's Code (c.1754 BC): goring ox (Laws 250–252), deposit of property (Laws 120–126), seduction of an unbetrothed woman (Law 130). The parallels suggest literary dependence or a common ancient Near Eastern legal tradition that Israel inherited.

positions.name: Independent composition reflecting common legal culture
positions.argument: The parallels reflect the common legal problems of agricultural societies (goring animals, property disputes, personal injury) that every ancient law code must address. The differences are as significant as the similarities: Israel's law is notably more egalitarian (same standard for all social classes) and more theologically grounded (law flows from covenant) than Hammurabi. Parallel structures do not require literary dependence.

coaching.tip: Restitution for theft varies: four sheep for one stolen, five oxen for one (v. 1). Why the difference? Oxen require more investment to replace—training, strength, utility. Justice accounts for economic reality.

'Do not mistreat or oppress a foreigner, for you were foreigners in Egypt' (v. 21). Memory shapes ethics. Israel's Egyptian experience becomes moral instruction: you know vulnerability; don't inflict it.

prayer_prompt: As you consider God's laws protecting the vulnerable — foreigners, widows, orphans — consider asking the Lord whether your definition of justice extends to the people most easily ignored in your community.
